bare: - Maras, K. L., & Bowler, D. M. (2014). "Eyewitness testimony in autism spectrum disorder: A review." *Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders.*
claim_context: Hub cites Maras & Bowler as foundational to the claim that autistic witnesses face credibility discounts and atypical performance in eyewitness scenarios.
source_says: Maras & Bowler (2014) JADD is a well-known review documenting how episodic memory and communicative differences in ASD affect eyewitness testimony reliability and courtroom outcomes.
claim_support: Directly supports the hub's claim that autistic individuals face structural disadvantages in eyewitness contexts due to memory/communication interaction effects.

key: maras|2019|brief report autism in the cou
bare: Maras, K., Crane, L., Mulcahy, S., Hawken, T., Cooper, P., Wurtzel, D., & Memon, A. (2019). Brief report: Autism in the courtroom: Experiences of legal professionals and the autism community. *Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders*, 47(8), 2610-2620. [Springer](https://doi.org/10.1007/s10803-017-3162-9)
claim_context: Supports claims that autistic people face pragmatic mismatch in court and legal contexts.
source_says: Maras et al. is a qualitative study with legal professionals and autistic community members documenting communication barriers and systemic issues in courtroom experiences.
claim_support: Supports the claim that courtroom pragmatic norms disadvantage autistic participants.
notes: Bibliographic year mismatch: hub lists 2019 but DOI 10.1007/s10803-017-3162-9 resolves to a 2017 JADD paper in 47(8). Content claim supported; year should be 2017.

bare: - Maras, K. et al. (2021). "Adapted Interview Questions for Autistic Adults." [PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8108109/)
claim_context: Supports claim that adapting interview question formats reduces autistic disadvantage in employment interviews.
source_says: PMC8108109 verified as Maras, Norris, Nicholson, Heasman, Remington & Crane (2021) Autism, showing adapted interview questions improved autistic candidates' evaluations.

bare: - Maras, K. et al. (2021). "Ameliorating the disadvantage for autistic job seekers: An initial evaluation of adapted employment interview questions." *Autism*, 25(4), 1060-1075. [PMC8108109](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8108109/)
claim_context: Documents structural disadvantage in standard employment interviews.
source_says: Verified Maras et al. 2021 Autism: standard questions disadvantage autistic candidates; adapted questions improve performance for both groups.

bare: - Marco et al. (2011). — [PMC3086654](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3086654/)
claim_context: Documents measurable neurophysiological sensory differences in autism, countering sensory-gaslighting.
source_says: Verified PMC3086654 = Marco, Hinkley, Hill & Nagarajan (2011) Pediatric Research review of neurophysiological sensory findings in ASD.
claim_support: Directly supports: sensory differences are objectively measurable at neural level.

bare: - Marco, E. J., et al. (2011). "Sensory processing in autism: A review of neurophysiologic findings." *Pediatric Research.* — [PMC3086654](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3086654/)
claim_context: Foundational neurophysiology of ASD sensory processing.
source_says: Verified Marco et al. 2011 Pediatric Research 69(5 Pt 2):48R-54R.

bare: - Marco et al. (2011). Sensory processing in autism. *Pediatric Research.* — [PMC3086654](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3086654/)
claim_context: Duplicate format variant.
source_says: Same Marco 2011 paper, verified.
notes: Housekeeping duplicate.

bare: - Marocchini, E. & Baldin, A. (2024). "Cross-neurotype communication from an autistic point of view." *International Journal of Language & Communication Disorders*. [Wiley](https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/1460-6984.13095)
claim_context: Supports mutual-manifestness / double-empathy framing — autistic-autistic communication easier than cross-neurotype.
source_says: Marocchini & Baldin (2024) IJLCD focus-group study with 23 autistic adults reporting smoother autistic-autistic communication.
claim_support: First-person autistic reports corroborate mutual-manifestness account.

bare: - Marocchini, E. (2026). "Intercultural Cognitive Pragmatics as a tool for understanding autism." *Frontiers in Human Neuroscience*. [Frontiers](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/human-neuroscience/articles/10.3389/fnhum.2026.1729076/full)
claim_context: Frames cross-neurotype communication as analogous to cross-cultural pragmatic work.
source_says: Frontiers in Human Neuroscience article by Marocchini applying intercultural cognitive pragmatics framework to autism.
claim_support: Supports intercultural framing of double empathy.
notes: 2026 forward-dated / in-press; URL not independently verified in this session.

bare: - Marra, M., Vine, B. & Holmes, J. (2008). "Workplace Interaction." In *Handbook of Pragmatics*. John Benjamins.
claim_context: Cited as foundational workplace-interaction chapter in Handbook of Pragmatics.
source_says: Marra, Vine & Holmes chapter from the Wellington Language in the Workplace project.
claim_support: Supports workplace pragmatic norms as a researched domain.

key: marwick|2011|i tweet honestly i tweet passi
bare: - Marwick, A.E. & boyd, d. (2011). "I tweet honestly, I tweet passionately: Twitter users, context collapse, and the imagined audience." *New Media & Society*, 13(1), 114-133.
claim_context: Canonical 'context collapse' concept — online audiences merge such that no single register fits.
source_says: Marwick & boyd 2011 is the canonical paper introducing context collapse and the imagined audience on Twitter.

bare: - Maryland Network Against Domestic Violence (2022). *Position Paper: Effectiveness of the Lethality Assessment Program.*
claim_context: Supports the Lethality Assessment Program (LAP) in DV safety planning.
source_says: MNADV publishes position papers on LAP effectiveness.
claim_support: Grey literature supporting LAP as evidence-based DV screening tool.
notes: Grey-lit; exact 2022 paper not independently verified.

bare: - Masia, V. (2020). *The Manipulative Disguise of Truth: Tricks and threats of implicit communication*. John Benjamins.
claim_context: Pragmatic accounts of implicit/covert manipulation.
source_says: Masia (2020) is a Benjamins monograph on implicature/presupposition as tools of manipulation.
claim_support: Supports theoretical framing that manipulation exploits implicit communication.

bare: - Mastroianni, A. M., Gilbert, D. T., Cooney, G., & Wilson, T. D. (2021). Do conversations end when people want them to? *PNAS*, 118(10).
claim_context: Normalizes that everyone struggles with conversation closings.
source_says: Verified: Mastroianni, Gilbert, Cooney & Wilson (2021) PNAS 118(10) e2011809118; conversations almost never end when participants want them to.
claim_support: Directly supports the claim that closings are hard for everyone.

bare: - Mast, M.S. (2002). "Dominance as Expressed and Inferred Through Speaking Time." *Human Communication Research*.
claim_context: Classic finding that speaking time signals dominance.
source_says: Mast (2002) Human Communication Research is the canonical speaking-time-as-dominance paper.

bare: - Mathesius, V. (1929). "Zur Satzperspektive im modernen Englisch." *Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen*, 155, 202-210. [CITATION UNVERIFIED — no stable online source]
claim_context: Historical origin of Functional Sentence Perspective.
source_says: Mathesius is the founder of Prague functionalism and FSP; the 1929 paper is historically real.
claim_support: Supports the historical FSP origin claim.
notes: Hub already flags 'CITATION UNVERIFIED' appropriately.

bare: - Matsuki, K. (1995). "Metaphors of anger in Japanese." In Taylor & MacLaury (eds.), *Language and the Cognitive Construal of the World*. Berlin: Mouton de Gruyter.
claim_context: Classic cross-linguistic work on ANGER IS HEAT metaphor in Japanese.
source_says: Matsuki chapter in Mouton de Gruyter volume is a well-known cognitive-linguistics comparative study.

bare: - Matsumoto, Y. (1988). "Reexamination of the Universality of Face: Politeness Phenomena in Japanese." *Journal of Pragmatics*, 12, 403-426.
claim_context: Canonical critique of B&L universalist face framework using Japanese data.
source_says: Matsumoto (1988) J. Pragmatics 12(4):403-426 argues Japanese face is relational/wakimae-oriented.
claim_support: Directly supports the critique of B&L universalism.

bare: - Maynard, D.W. (1980). "Placement of Topic Changes in Conversation." *Semiotica*, 30(3-4), 263-290.
claim_context: Classic CA work on topic-change placement.
source_says: Maynard 1980 Semiotica is well-known CA work on topic placement.

bare: - Maynard, D.W. (2003). *Bad News, Good News: Conversational Order in Everyday Talk and Clinical Settings*. University of Chicago Press.
claim_context: Canonical book on delivery sequences for bad/good news.
source_says: Maynard 2003, U. Chicago Press, well-known monograph on news-delivery order.

bare: 9. Mazzaggio, G., Foppolo, F., Job, R. & Surian, L. (2021). Ad-hoc and scalar implicatures in children with ASD. *Journal of Communication Disorders*. PubMed: 33621945.
claim_context: Supports dissociation: ToM predicts scalar but not ad-hoc implicature performance in autistic children.
source_says: Verified: Mazzaggio, Foppolo, Job & Surian (2021) J. Communication Disorders 90. 26 ASD + 26 TD children; ToM linked to scalar but not ad-hoc.

bare: - Mazzaggio et al. (2021): ToM predicted scalar implicature performance but NOT ad-hoc implicature performance in autistic children
claim_context: Same ToM/scalar/ad-hoc dissociation claim.
source_says: Same verified Mazzaggio et al. 2021.

bare: - Mazzaggio et al. (2021). Understanding indirect requests for information. *Cognitive Processing*. PMC8831260, PubMed: 34487273.
claim_context: Supports claim that conventionalized indirect requests are processed well by autistic adults but hints are harder.
source_says: Verified PMC8831260 = Marocchini, Di Paola, Mazzaggio & Domaneschi (2021) Cognitive Processing. Mazzaggio is co-author.
notes: Lead author is Marocchini, not Mazzaggio. 'Mazzaggio et al.' is not standard citation practice; first-author citation (Marocchini et al.) would be more accurate. Note: Slices 1-7 flagged the same PMC as 'actually Marocchini et al.' — content claim still stands.

bare: - McAdams, D. P. (2001). The psychology of life stories. *Review of General Psychology*, 5(2), 100–122.
claim_context: Canonical narrative-identity framework.
source_says: McAdams 2001 Review of General Psychology is the canonical statement of narrative identity theory.

bare: - McCann, J., & Peppe, S. (2003). Prosody in autism spectrum disorders: a critical review. *IJLCD 38*(4), 325-350.

source_says: McCann & Peppé 2003 IJLCD 38(4):325-350 is a well-known critical review.

bare: - McCann, J., Peppé, S., Gibbon, F.E., O'Hare, A. & Rutherford, M. (2007). "Prosody and its Relationship to Language in School-Aged Children with High-Functioning Autism." *IJLCD*, 42(6), 682-702.

key: mccarthy|2004|there s millions of them hyper
bare: - McCarthy, M. & Carter, R. (2004). "There's millions of them": hyperbole in everyday conversation. *Journal of Pragmatics*, 36, 149-184.
claim_context: Corpus evidence that hyperbole is pervasive in everyday talk.
source_says: McCarthy & Carter 2004 J. Pragmatics is the well-known corpus study of hyperbole in English conversation.

key: mcclanahan|2022|two ways to stay at the top pr
bare: - McClanahan, K.J., Maner, J.K. & Cheng, J.T. (2022). "Two Ways to Stay at the Top." *Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin*.
claim_context: Supports dual-strategies theory: prestige AND dominance both viable for social rank.
source_says: Verified: McClanahan, Maner & Cheng (2022) PSPB 48(11):1516-1528. Both prestige and dominance predict rank gains over 4 weeks.

bare: - McCulloch, G. (2019). *Because Internet: Understanding the New Rules of Language*. Riverhead Books.
claim_context: Popular but rigorous account of digital language pragmatics (emoji, tone indicators).
source_says: Well-known popular linguistics book on internet language.

bare: - McDonald, L. (2023). "Flirting." In *The Oxford Handbook of Philosophy of Love*.
claim_context: Cites flirting as canonical off-record indirect speech act.
source_says: Oxford Handbook chapter on flirting by McDonald applies speech-act theory to flirtation.
claim_support: Supports treating flirting as off-record indirect speech act.

bare: - McGraw, A. P. & Warren, C. (2010). Benign Violations: Making Immoral Behavior Funny. *Psychological Science*, 21(8), 1141-1149.
claim_context: Canonical Benign Violation Theory of humor.
source_says: McGraw & Warren 2010 Psychological Science is the canonical BVT introduction.

key: mcneill|1992|hand and mind what gestures re
bare: - McNeill, D. (1992). *Hand and Mind: What Gestures Reveal About Thought*. University of Chicago Press.

bare: - McNeill, D. (2005). *Gesture and Thought*. University of Chicago Press.

bare: - McSweeney, M.A. (2018). *The Pragmatics of Text Messaging: Making Meaning in Messages*. Routledge.

bare: Medina, J. (2012). *The Epistemology of Resistance*. Oxford University Press.

bare: - Mehan, H. (1979). *Learning Lessons*. Harvard University Press.
claim_context: Canonical work on IRE/IRF classroom question sequences.

bare: - Miller, D., Rees, J., & Pearson, A. (2021). "Masking is life". *Autism in Adulthood*, 3(4), 330-338.

key: milton|2012|on the ontological status of a
bare: - Milton, D. (2012). "On the ontological status of autism: the 'double empathy problem'." *Disability & Society*, 27(6), 883-887.
claim_context: THE load-bearing paper for this hub's framing of pragmatic mismatch as bidirectional (the 'double empathy problem').
source_says: Milton (2012) Disability & Society 27(6):883-887 coined the double empathy problem.
claim_support: Directly supports the hub's central framing across dozens of files.

bare: - Milton, D. (2012). "The Double Empathy Problem." *Disability & Society*, 27(6).
claim_context: Same canonical Milton 2012 reference, hub-level summary variants.
source_says: Same verified paper.
notes: Duplicate of main Milton 2012 entry.

bare: - Milton, D., Gurbuz, E., & López, B. (2022). "The 'double empathy problem': Ten years on." *Autism* 26(8): 1901–1903.
claim_context: Milton's 2022 retrospective editorial reviewing a decade of double-empathy research.
source_says: Milton, Gurbuz & López 2022 Autism 26(8):1901-1903 is the well-known 10-year retrospective editorial.
claim_support: Directly supports growing evidence base claim.

bare: Mitchell, P., Sheppard, E., & Cassidy, S. (2021). Autism and the double empathy problem: Implications for development and mental health. *British Journal of Developmental Psychology*, 39(1), 1-18.
claim_context: Extends double-empathy to mental health / developmental outcomes.
source_says: Verified: Mitchell, Sheppard & Cassidy (2021) BJDP 39(1):1-18 links double empathy to mental-health risk.

key: mitchell|2021|extended the line of work argu
bare: - Mitchell, Sheppard, & Cassidy (2021) extended the line of work, arguing that 'autistic social impairment' is better characterized as an interaction-level mismatch.
claim_context: Same paper, glossed mention.
source_says: Same verified paper.

bare: - Mizuno, A. et al. (2011). "Neural Basis of Deictic Shifting in Linguistic Perspective-Taking in High-Functioning Autism." [PMC3155703](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3155703/)
claim_context: Supports neural-level evidence of deictic-shifting differences in autism.
source_says: Verified PMC3155703 = Mizuno, Liu, Williams, Keller, Minshew & Just (2011). JOURNAL IS 'Brain' 134(8):2422-2435, NOT Neuropsychologia.
notes: Journal misattribution in other hub citation variants: some list Neuropsychologia, but the actual paper is in Brain.

bare: - [Mizuno et al. (2011) (PMC3155703)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3155703/)

key: mizuno|2011|the neural basis of deictic sh
bare: - Mizuno, A. et al. (2011). "The neural basis of deictic shifting in linguistic perspective-taking in high-functioning autism." *Neuropsychologia*, 49(10), 2743-2751. [PMC3155703](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3155703/)
claim_context: Neural-deictic-shifting claim, full bibliographic form.
source_says: Same paper BUT journal/volume/pagination WRONG: actual paper is Brain 134(8):2422-2435, NOT Neuropsychologia 49(10):2743-2751.
claim_support: Content claim supported by the real paper.
notes: JOURNAL MISATTRIBUTION — hub lists Neuropsychologia 49(10):2743-2751 but PMC3155703 is actually Brain 134(8):2422-2435. Same underlying paper, wrong journal metadata.

bare: - Monk, A. (2003). Common ground in electronically mediated communication. *Synthesis Lectures on Human-Centered Informatics*.

source_says: Monk is a well-known HCI researcher; Synthesis Lectures treatments exist.

bare: - Morand, D.A. (1996). "Dominance, Deference, and Egalitarianism in Organizational Interaction." *Organization Science*, 7(5), 544-556.
claim_context: Supports claim that organizational hierarchies shape pragmatic choices.
source_says: Morand 1996 Organization Science is a known paper on pragmatic markers of organizational hierarchy.

bare: - Morrison et al. (2020) and DeBrabander et al. (2019) showed that autistic-autistic pairs produced outcomes different from mixed pairs even on the same task content.
claim_context: Supports neurotype-matching effect.
source_says: Morrison et al. 2020 verified; autistic-autistic pairs showed distinct profiles.

bare: - Morrison et al. (2020) on first impressions between autistic and non-autistic participants.
claim_context: First impressions / rapport.
source_says: Morrison et al. 2020 covers real-world social interaction outcomes including impressions.

bare: Morrison, K. E., DeBrabander, K. M., Jones, D. R., Faso, D. J., Ackerman, R. A., & Sasson, N. J. (2020). Outcomes of real-world social interaction for autistic adults paired with autistic compared to typically developing partners. *Autism*, 24(5), 1067-1080.
claim_context: Empirical support for neurotype-matching / double-empathy.
source_says: Verified: Morrison et al. (2020) Autism 24(5):1067-1080. ASD-ASD pairs prefer each other; NT partners prefer NT partners.

bare: - Morsanyi, K., Stamenković, D. & Holyoak, K. J. (2020). "Analogical reasoning in autism: A systematic review and meta-analysis." In Morsanyi & Holyoak (eds.), *Thinking, Reasoning, and Decision Making in Autism*. Routledge.

bare: Morsanyi et al. (2022) directly linked metaphor comprehension with analogical reasoning.

bare: - Morsanyi, K., Stamenković, D. & Holyoak, K. J. (2022). "Linking metaphor comprehension with analogical reasoning." *British Journal of Psychology* 113(2):479–495.

bare: - [Nadig, Vivanti & Ozonoff (2009) - Adaptation of Object Descriptions in ASD](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/19998354/)
claim_context: Supports common-ground / audience design claims in ASD.
source_says: Verified: Nadig, Vivanti & Ozonoff (2009) Autism Research, 'Adaptation of object descriptions to a partner under increasing communicative demands' — referential communication task comparing ASD and TD school-aged children.

key: nadig|2009|the art of common ground emerg
bare: - Nadig, A., Vivanti, G. & Ozonoff, S. (2009). [The art of common ground: emergence of a complex pragmatic language skill in adolescents with ASD](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4764348/). -- Common ground and referential shortening study
claim_context: Hub attributes 'The art of common ground' paper to Nadig/Vivanti/Ozonoff (2009) via PMC4764348.
source_says: CRITICAL MISATTRIBUTION: PMC4764348 is actually 'The art of common ground: emergence of a complex pragmatic language skill in adolescents with autism spectrum disorders' by Ashley de Marchena and Inge-Marie Eigsti (2015) in Journal of Child Language — NOT Nadig/Vivanti/Ozonoff 2009.
claim_support: Content claim about common ground IS supported by PMC4764348, but the authorship and year are wrong. Nadig, Vivanti & Ozonoff (2009) is a real but DIFFERENT paper (Autism Research, object-description adaptation).
notes: AUTHOR/TITLE MISATTRIBUTION — matches the known error mode (slices 1-7 found the same PMC4764348 mis-attributed to Hyter 2017). The real authors of 'The art of common ground' paper are de Marchena & Eigsti (2015). Hub is conflating two papers.

bare: - Naigles, L. et al. (2016). "Prevalence and Predictors of Pronoun Reversals in ASD." [PMC4834724](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4834724/)
claim_context: Supports claim that pronoun reversal in ASD is real but low-rate and predicted by language/joint-attention.
source_says: Verified: PMC4834724 = Naigles, Cheng, Xu Rattansone, Tek, Khetrapal, Fein & Demuth (2016) 'You're telling me!' Research in Autism Spectrum Disorders. Rates <10% generally.

bare: 13. O'Nions, E., Christie, P., Gould, J., Viding, E., & Happé, F. (2014). "Development of the 'Extreme Demand Avoidance Questionnaire' (EDA-Q)." *JCPP*, 55(7), 758-768. PMC4820467.

source_says: CRITICAL MISATTRIBUTION: PMC4820467 is actually O'Nions, Gould, Christie, Gillberg, Viding & Happé (2015) 'Identifying features of pathological demand avoidance using the DISCO' in European Child & Adolescent Psychiatry — NOT the 2014 EDA-Q paper in JCPP. Both papers exist but are different.
claim_support: The 2014 EDA-Q paper is real (O'Nions et al. 2014 JCPP 55(7):758-768), but the PMC4820467 URL points to a DIFFERENT O'Nions paper (the 2015 DISCO paper). Hub has conflated two papers.
notes: AUTHOR/TITLE/YEAR MISATTRIBUTION in PMC link: bibliographic info (year 2014, title EDA-Q, journal JCPP) correctly names the real 2014 paper, BUT the attached PMC4820467 link resolves to the separate 2015 DISCO paper. This is a URL error.

claim_context: Supports claim that autistic adults prefer short-gap transitions in conversation but achieve them only after a warm-up period.

Ochi et al. did publish a 2019 PLOS One paper on autistic conversation speech/synchrony, which is a different study.
claim_support: The content claim IS supported by the real Wehrle et al. 2023 paper, but the authorship attribution is WRONG.
notes: AUTHOR MISATTRIBUTION: The hub cites 'Ochi et al. 2023' for a paper that is actually by Wehrle et al. 2023. Ochi has related prior work (2019) but is not an author of the PLOS One 2023 turn-timing paper.

bare: 1. Ogawa, S., Iriguchi, M., Lee, Y.A. et al. (2019). "Atypical Social Rank Recognition in Autism Spectrum Disorder." *Scientific Reports*, 9, 15657.
claim_context: Supports empirical claim that autistic children show atypical social rank recognition.
source_says: Verified: Ogawa et al. (2019) Scientific Reports 9:15657. Paper reports ASD children showed MORE robust, not impaired, social rank recognition — with potential neural compensation hypothesis.
claim_support: Supports the claim about atypical rank processing, though the finding may be more nuanced than 'hierarchy blindness' framing — ASD group showed robust but atypical recognition.
notes: Finding is more complex than simple deficit: Ogawa reports MORE robust, atypical recognition, not absence. Hub should ensure 'hierarchy blindness' framing correctly represents this.
